summary:
The Sykes-Picot Agreement was a secret diplomatic accord concluded in May 1916 between Britain and France, negotiated by British diplomat Mark Sykes and French diplomat François Georges-Picot, with Russian assent, that divided the Arab territories of the declining Ottoman Empire into British and French spheres of influence. Britain was to control what is now Iraq and Jordan, and have indirect influence over the Arabian Peninsula; France was to control what is now Syria and Lebanon, and have indirect influence further east; Russia was promised control of Constantinople and the Turkish Straits, an arrangement that became moot after the Russian Revolution.

The agreement conflicted with promises Britain had made to Arab leaders, particularly Sharif Hussein of Mecca, who had been encouraged to launch the Arab Revolt against the Ottomans in return for British support for Arab independence. It also conflicted with the subsequent [[event:2752|Balfour Declaration]] of 1917 regarding a Jewish national home in Palestine. When the Bolsheviks revealed the agreement's contents in 1917, it caused significant embarrassment and damaged Arab trust in British intentions.

The Sykes-Picot Agreement effectively established the boundaries of the modern Middle East. The borders drawn by British and French administrators after the war — often cutting across ethnic, religious, and tribal boundaries without reference to local populations — created states whose artificiality and the resulting sectarian tensions contributed to instability that has persisted throughout the twentieth and twenty-first centuries.
